business_info: Stable contracts are those with good credit performance.

Premium clients are segmented into three behavioral cohorts based on card usage, but this segmentation is not directly exposed in the database; the internal cohorts are labeled "emerging premium", "core premium", and "elite premium" and are derived from a mix of card type, average monthly spend, and tenure.

Marketing sometimes refers to "premium" in a broader sense than the card_type column, using internal propensity scores and lifestyle indicators; analysts should be aware that not all customers labeled as premium in campaign decks will have a premium card product in the core systems.

Urban regions in our strategic planning documents do not always align with national statistical definitions; in some legacy reports, a branch is classified as urban simply if it is within a 45-minute commute to a city center, regardless of the underlying district-level urbanization ratios.

For internal dashboards, "stable contracts" are sometimes proxied using a composite risk band that combines loan performance, overdraft behavior, and card delinquency; this composite band is maintained outside the core transactional database and may not be reproducible from the standard tables alone.

The term "operational activity" has been used inconsistently across projects; in some risk models it refers strictly to payment transactions, while in customer analytics it can also include non-monetary events such as logins, limit changes, and address updates captured in auxiliary systems.

Clients may hold multiple cards over their lifecycle; when a card is upgraded from standard to gold, historical reporting sometimes still counts earlier activity under the premium segment for continuity of time series analysis.

The internal age banding scheme for customer analytics uses non-standard cutoffs (e.g., 0–20, 21–27, 28–35, 36–49, 50+), which do not correspond exactly to legal or regulatory definitions of minors or seniors; this is mainly a marketing convention.

Some historical campaigns offered "youth premium" packages with enhanced card features for younger clients; these products used the same gold card plastic but had different pricing and eligibility rules, and they were discontinued several years ago.

District-level socio-demographic scores are periodically refreshed using external census data; in practice, front-line segmentation sometimes lags behind these updates, resulting in branches being treated as semi-urban or rural in operational planning even if the underlying district metrics suggest high urbanization.

Contracts originated via digital channels sometimes have incomplete regional coding at inception; until the district mapping is resolved, these customers can appear under a generic "unassigned" region in certain extracts, regardless of their true urban or rural status.

Operational activity scores in CRM are built from multiple sources including card transactions, loan payments, and contact center interactions; the exact weighting scheme is proprietary and may change over time without direct documentation in the database.

In some analytical sandboxes, stable contracts are approximated using a minimum vintage requirement (e.g., at least 24 months since origination) combined with absence of write-offs, but this heuristic is not part of the official risk policy.

Region classifications in the reporting layer sometimes use a three-tier scheme (urban, mixed, rural) based on internal scoring cutoffs that were calibrated years ago; documentation of the exact cutoffs is incomplete and may differ from those implied by the raw district table.

For customer experience studies, "operationally active" clients are those who interacted with any channel (branch, ATM, digital, or call center) at least once in the last 90 days; this definition is broader than purely transactional metrics.

Some premium card propositions require a minimum monthly income threshold, but exceptions can be granted by relationship managers for family accounts; in such cases, dependent cardholders may appear with premium products even if they do not meet the usual standalone eligibility criteria.

Family banking offerings allow a single premium relationship to cover multiple related clients across generations; analytical views sometimes assign the relationship status to all members, which can blur the distinction between primary and secondary premium clients.

When analyzing minors, regulatory reporting usually distinguishes between legal minors and "financial minors"—customers who formally have capacity but are treated with additional protections due to low income, low experience, or vulnerability; these categories are configured in compliance systems rather than core account tables.

To protect privacy, certain age fields in analytical extracts are binned or truncated (e.g., birth years grouped into three-year buckets) so point-in-time age may not be recoverable exactly; this can affect precise minor/majority determinations.

Some branches located in suburban belts are coded under urban marketing clusters but fall under non-urban administrative districts; cross-referencing branch codes with district codes is necessary to untangle these discrepancies in region-based analysis.

Operational activity flags in some BI cubes are updated overnight based on the previous day's transactions only, which means that real-time extracts from core systems may not align with the latest cube-based activity indicators.

In certain risk monitoring packs, "stable" is not a binary label but a score band on a 1–9 internal stability scale, where bands 1–3 are considered unstable, 4–6 transitional, and 7–9 stable; the mapping from this scale to contract-level attributes is not fully documented.

Premium relationship definitions sometimes depend on total assets under management and not just card products; customers without any cards can still be flagged as premium in wealth management systems based on their investment holdings.

Urban/rural designations used in branch incentive schemes are frozen at the start of each fiscal year; mid-year changes in district urbanization metrics are not back-propagated to these calculations, which can cause differences between incentive reports and ad hoc analytics.

For operational KPIs, management defines "highly active" accounts using a combination of transaction count, ATM usage, and digital session frequency, but the exact parameter values can differ between retail and SME segments.

Historic product catalogs included several overlapping tiers of gold-like cards (e.g., Gold Classic, Gold Plus, Affluent Gold), which may all be collapsed into a single 'gold' value in simplified analytical datasets.

Some legacy loan systems use different status code sets than the main loan table, and nightly ETL jobs map these into the unified status values; edge cases in this mapping can occasionally classify borderline contracts as performing even when they are close to default.

Customer lifecycle analyses often group accounts into phases (onboarding, growth, maturity, decline) based on patterns of operational activity and balance trends; the phase boundaries are recalibrated periodically and are not stored in the core schema.

For youth and student propositions, parental or guardian consent is often recorded in separate document management systems, so the presence of a minor in the customer table does not by itself indicate the nature of the contractual relationship.

Internal region hierarchies sometimes overlay district-based segmentation with custom sales territories; these hierarchies can split a single district across multiple territories, complicating any attempt to attribute performance to a single urban region label.
